title: Ibrahim Traoré Would Like to Be Thomas Sankara’s Heir

title: “I’m Running Because It Shouldn’t Be So Hard to Live Here”

title: The AI Revolution Could Usher In a New Age of Stagnation
summary: Critics of generative AI have for the most part been obsessed with a single question: What if the several hundred billion–dollar bet on the future of the world economy fails? This isn’t just a concern about the benefits of the technology. Bottlenecks exist at seemingly every stage. Energy supply is severely constrained by regional war […]

title: Anti-Imperialism and Its Fault Lines
summary: In the early 1920s, the Latin American landscape was rocked by two political earthquakes. Though different in nature, the Mexican and Russian Revolutions shared much in common: domestically, both fought for the cause of social justice, while abroad both raised the flag of sovereignty against imperialist interests. Most important of all, the triumph of the […]

title: Victor Serge Was One of the Great Revolutionary Writers
summary: Many readers will be familiar with Victor Serge’s literary work: his novels, notably The Case of Comrade Tulayev, and his fascinating autobiography Memoirs of a Revolutionary. All his work centers around the great historical events of the first half of the twentieth century, the hopes aroused by the Russian Revolution of 1917, and its subsequent […]
reason: A literary retrospective on a historical revolutionary writer is representational content — new framing of an old figure — rather than an analysis that illuminates current capture mechanisms or commons-building.

title: LA Socialists’ Debates Reflect the Left’s Growing Strength
summary: On a late March afternoon, beneath the vaulted, medieval-revival ceiling of Immanuel Presbyterian Church, more than four hundred members of the Los Angeles chapter of the Democratic Socialists of America (DSA) gathered in the lingering heat of a citywide heat wave. The air inside the sanctuary was thick and stubborn as members fanned themselves with […]
reason: A 400-person chapter meeting debating organizational direction is a concrete routing-architecture moment — how does a mass left organization structure decision-making and resource flow — but the Jacobin framing ('growing strength') risks reducing it to representational politics.
angle: Use the routing architecture tool to ask what the DSA chapter debate actually routes — attention, resources, candidate pipelines, mutual aid — versus what it represents, and whether internal democratic process is itself a prefigurative institution or a simulation of one.

title: Scientists Gave a Bunch of Salmon Cocaine. This Is What Happened Next.
summary: Salmon exposed to cocaine and its byproduct swam farther than unexposed fish, raising alarms about drug pollution in aquatic ecosystems.
reason: Interesting environmental science but primarily a content-level curiosity story with no meaningful purchase for the framework's core concepts.

title: Forbes Prediction Market Gamifies Story About Mass Shooting of 8 Children
summary: Forbes launched ForbesPredict in January as part of an effort to reverse declining traffic from search engines and keep users on its website longer.

title: Why Journalists Are Going Indie (with Maddy Myers)
summary: Maddy and Sam get into the launch of Mothership and the importance of owning one's own work.
reason: Journalists building independent publishing infrastructure is a concrete example of routing-level change — rerouting attention and revenue flows away from corporate media platforms toward self-owned productive circuits.
angle: Use the routing architecture tool to examine how indie journalist collectives like Mothership shift who controls the pipes, not just the content — and where that structural move succeeds or gets recaptured by platform dependency.

title: In two months, Trump’s Cabinet has lost three women
summary: President Donald Trump’s second Cabinet was never exceptionally diverse from the start. Then, less than a month later, Trump ousted […]
reason: This is primarily a representational story about personnel diversity metrics in an elite institution, adequately explained by simpler political analysis without requiring the framework.

summary: The post Trump Pardoned a Nursing Home Owner Who Owed Almost $19 Million to a Grieving Family appeared first on ProPublica .

title: The Rupture at Hand
summary: Facing down a billionaire-backed authoritarian blitz, the Left isn't waiting for Democratic elites to join step with the party's base.

title: After Decades of Quiet Rumbling, an Epidemic Is Erupting Among California Stoneworkers
summary: The engineered stone industry is worth nearly $30 billion. But the workers at its core are falling sick and dying from an illness called silicosis. Now these workers—most of whom are young immigrants—are suing manufacturers.

title: Saving Culture at the Beeb
summary: The BBC is a key employer and a major creator of arts and entertainment, but neither role appears to matter yet in the ongoing review of its charter. That charter — the constitutional basis for the corporation, setting out its public purpose and governance — is now under review, with the government’s Green Paper published […]
reason: The BBC charter review illuminates how a public commons institution gets re-routed through market and state capture mechanisms, with its alibi (public purpose) becoming visible under pressure.
angle: Use the alibi structure tool: the BBC's 'public purpose' framing has functioned as legitimation for the corporation's existence, and the charter review reveals how that alibi is being renegotiated to serve routing interests that have nothing to do with the common it supposedly protects.

title: Tuesday’s Headlines Curb Their Enthusiasm
summary: Curbs: They're not just for parking anymore.
reason: This is a low-content headline aggregator item about urban curb space with no substantive analytical hook for the framework.

title: ‘Best Bikeshare in America’: An Unexpected Community Launches Free, All-Electric Micromobility For Residents
summary: Omaha and neighboring Council Bluffs, Iowa share an expansive e-bikeshare network that punches above its weight, supporters say — and now, it's free to all residents, too.
reason: A municipal e-bikeshare network going free and cross-border demonstrates concrete routing-level infrastructure that bypasses the extractive fare/platform model, showing prefigurative mobility commons in practice.
angle: Use the routing architecture tool to show how eliminating the payment barrier changes who moves through the city and what connections become possible — a structural shift, not just a content-level subsidy.

title: ‘A Solution, But To What Problem?’ Experts Say AVs Are The Elephant In The Room, But There’s Still Time To Figure Out Their Role
summary: Want to know more about autonomous vehicles? Read this vital excerpt from last week's "The Future of Transportation" seminar.
reason: A seminar recap about AVs is primarily informational content with no clear capture mechanism, routing question, or commune layer illuminated.

title: When Traffic Violence Hits The Same Family Twice — Years Apart, On Exactly the Same Street
summary: The deaths of a Colorado married couple has some mourning an eerie coincidence — and others outraged at two predictable tragedies that could have been prevented.
reason: A compelling human story about infrastructure failure, but as summarized it stays at the level of personal tragedy and advocacy messaging rather than illuminating a specific capture or routing mechanism.

title: Monday’s Headlines Should Wean Themselves Off Fossil Fuels
summary: Even people who don't drive wind up paying when oil prices spike.

title: Why UBI is making a comeback
summary: Tech companies hope a check in the mail will calm the AI backlash — but there are reasons for skepticism
reason: This story directly illustrates aesthetic capture and alibi structure: UBI is being recruited as the legitimating story that allows AI-driven displacement to proceed without routing-level resistance.
angle: Use alibi structure — the UBI proposal is what tech capital needs you to believe in order for mass automation to seem acceptable, transforming a routing question (who controls the productive infrastructure?) into a content question (how big is the check?).
